title: Madagascar's Military Leader Mandates Polygraph Tests for New Ministers
description: Interim President Michael Randrianirina requires cabinet hopefuls to pass lie detector tests to combat corruption, following the unexplained dismissal of his previous government.
lede: Madagascar's interim military president, Michael Randrianirina, has mandated that all new ministerial candidates must pass a lie detector test before taking office. The sweeping decree follows his abrupt dismissal of the prime minister and entire cabinet earlier this month, a move he claims will root out entrenched political corruption.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Interim President Michael Randrianirina announced that the use of a polygraph will be standard protocol for background integrity checks on prospective cabinet members. The military ruler stated the unconventional measure is necessary to determine who is corrupt and who will uphold the ideals of the youth struggle that brought him to power.

The decree comes weeks after Randrianirina fired his prime minister and cabinet on March 9 without providing a public explanation. He subsequently appointed anti-corruption chief Mamitiana Rajaonarison as the new prime minister.

According to Randrianirina, he and Rajaonarison will only interview ministerial candidates who successfully pass the lie detector test. Speaking to local media, the president noted that the administration is 'not looking for someone who is 100% clean, but over 60%,' suggesting this threshold is sufficient to allow the nation to develop.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The mandate represents a highly unusual approach to government vetting in a nation struggling with deep-seated institutional corruption. Madagascar ranked 148 out of 180 countries in Transparency International's 2025 corruption perceptions index.

The country remains one of the world's poorest, with a GDP per capita of just $545 in 2024 according to World Bank data. Despite its wealth in natural resources like vanilla and precious gems, campaigners have long argued these assets are routinely exploited by corrupt officials and business interests rather than benefiting the population.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Randrianirina assumed power during an October coup that followed weeks of youth-led protests organized under the banner 'Gen Z Madagascar'. The protests initially sparked in September over severe water and power cuts before escalating into demands for a complete political overhaul, resulting in at least 22 deaths according to the UN.

On October 11, the elite military unit Capsat, where Randrianirina served as a colonel, backed the protesters. The following day, former President Andry Rajoelina reportedly fled to Dubai on a French military plane.

Despite his origins in the youth movement, young activists quickly grew disenchanted with Randrianirina's initial government appointments, viewing them as remnants of the old, corrupt elite. The polygraph mandate appears to be an attempt to restore credibility with the protest movement.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: A new cabinet is expected to be announced early next week, providing the first test of the polygraph vetting system. Observers will be closely watching whether the new appointments satisfy the demands of the youth movement or further alienate them.

Representatives of the 'Gen Z Madagascar' movement have already expressed skepticism regarding the polygraph tests, calling the unscientific method a joke and embarrassing. However, they maintain that the current regime is an improvement over Rajoelina's government.

Randrianirina, sworn in as interim president, has pledged to hold democratic elections by late 2027. Activists continue to pressure him to confirm a specific date while scrutinizing his transitional administration.
